1.synthesis:
The priestly garments were the final major production task. The skilled craftsmen made the service garments of blue, purple, and crimson used to cover the sacred objects during transport (Exod 39:1), then turned to the high-priestly vestments. The ephod was woven with gold wire twisted into the blue, purple, and crimson threads of fine linen, with shoulder pieces of onyx stones engraved with the tribal names in gold filigree settings (Exod 39:2-7). The breastpiece of judgment — a double pouch of gold-woven linen, set with twelve gems bearing the twelve tribal names — was attached to the ephod by chains and rings of gold and blue cord (Exod 39:8-21). The blue robe with its bell-and-pomegranate hem came next (Exod 39:22-26), then the finely woven tunics, turbans, and sashes for Aaron and his sons (Exod 39:27-29), and finally the golden plate inscribed "Holy to the Lord" for Aaron's turban (Exod 39:30-31).

Matthew Henry notes that the garments "are called here clothes of service" (Exod 39:1) — not ornaments, not status symbols, but functional instruments of priestly work. The beauty of the garments served their purpose; the purpose of the garments was service. "Holiness is the beauty of the spiritual priesthood," Henry writes, applying the garments' "glory and beauty" to the church's calling: clothed in Christ's righteousness, the royal priesthood serves with both dignity and function.

The consistent refrain of this chapter — "as the Lord had commanded Moses" — appears no fewer than seven times in the account of the garments' making (vv 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31). No other chapter in the construction narrative has this density of obedience-affirmation. The making of the garments was the most detail-dependent of all the tabernacle's construction: each piece had to fit precisely, each stone had to be set correctly, each bell had to ring in sequence. The seven-fold "as commanded" declares that the craftsmen met this standard completely.
1.historical_context: The garment production involved multiple specialized crafts: gold-wire twisting and weaving into fabric, gem-cutting and engraving, filigree-setting in gold, complex dyeing with blue-purple-crimson colors, and fine linen weaving. Each was a distinct ancient trade. The production timeline of roughly five months for all the tabernacle's furnishings and garments suggests either a large workshop with many craftsmen or extraordinary productivity. The golden plate inscribed "Holy to the Lord" (v 30) required both the metal-smithing skill to produce the plate and the engraving skill to inscribe the sacred Hebrew letters — the highest level of craftsmanship concentrated in the most visible single object the high priest wore.
1.literary_structure: Chapter 39's garment-making account mirrors chapter 28's garment instructions in both sequence and detail: ephod (39:2-7 // 28:6-14), breastpiece (39:8-21 // 28:15-30), robe (39:22-26 // 28:31-35), tunics and turbans (39:27-29 // 28:39), golden plate (39:30-31 // 28:36-38). The seven-fold "as the Lord commanded Moses" creates a structural signature unique to this chapter, marking the garments' production as the most explicitly obedience-confirmed of all the tabernacle components.
1.theological_application: The seven-fold "as the Lord commanded Moses" in the garments chapter speaks to the precision required in worship that is truly centered on God rather than on the worshipper's preferences. Each element of priestly dress had a function — covering, adorning, declaring, protecting — and each needed to be exactly right. The church's "garments" — the ordinances and disciplines of worship, the patterns of prayer and preaching — are not adiaphora to be shaped by cultural convenience but have a form received from God. The "as commanded" principle guards against both minimalism (ignoring what God prescribed) and maximalism (adding what God did not prescribe). Both errors distort the worship that the priestly garments were designed to enable.
1.christology: The seven-fold repetition of "as the Lord had commanded Moses" in this chapter — more than in any other — anticipates Jesus's declaration: "I have kept my Father's commandments and abide in his love" (John 15:10). The priestly garments made in exact obedience point to the true high priest who fulfilled the Father's will at every point. The golden plate "Holy to the Lord" on the high priest's forehead — worn perpetually — finds its fulfillment in Christ, of whom the Father declared at his baptism and transfiguration, "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased" (Matt 17:5). He is the "Holy to the Lord" in person, not merely in inscription.

32.synthesis:
"Thus all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of meeting was finished" (Exod 39:32). This declarative sentence is the construction narrative's climactic moment. The people of Israel "had done it" — they had made everything "according to all that the Lord had commanded Moses" — and they brought the completed tabernacle to Moses: "the tabernacle, the tent with all its utensils, its hooks, its frames, its bars, its pillars, its bases; the ark of the testimony with its poles and the mercy seat... the lampstand... the golden altar, the anointing oil and the fragrant incense, and the screen for the entrance of the tent; the bronze altar with its grating of bronze, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin and its stand; the hangings of the court, its pillars, and its bases... all the utensils for the service of the tabernacle" (Exod 39:33-40).

Moses inspected everything. He "saw all the work, and behold, they had done it; as the Lord had commanded, so had they done it" (Exod 39:43). Then Moses blessed them.

Matthew Henry sees in Moses's inspection and blessing a figure of Christ's evaluation and approval of his church: "When the tabernacle was erected and all the work done, Moses, like a master-builder, surveyed it, compared it with the pattern, and pronounced it good." The pattern had been given; the work had been executed; the inspector confirmed the correspondence. This is the narrative logic of the creation account in Genesis 1 — "and God saw that it was good" — repeated here in the construction of the dwelling where God would take up residence among his people. The Creator who called creation good now looked upon the craftsmen's work and called it good.

Moses's blessing of Israel at the completion of the tabernacle is the act of a mediator whose intercession has been answered. He had prayed for God's presence to accompany the people; the tabernacle is the material evidence that the prayer was granted. The blessing pronounced over completed, faithful work is a participation in the divine delight that awaits those who have been "faithful over a little" — and for whom the Master will say, "Enter into the joy of your master" (Matt 25:21).
32.historical_context: Matthew Henry's note that the tabernacle was completed in approximately five months — "not much more than five months" from start to finish — indicates extraordinary productivity given the technical demands of the project. The presentation of all completed items to Moses for inspection (vv 33-43) before any assembly took place reflects an organized project-management procedure: complete all components first, then present them for review, then assemble. Moses's blessing of the congregation (v 43) echoes the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26, though here it is the mediator blessing the community that faithfully completed the divine commission, not yet the formal Aaronic blessing of ongoing sanctuary service.
32.literary_structure: The completion declaration (v 32) opens the unit; the inspection (vv 33-43a) follows as a long catalogue of every completed item; the blessing (v 43b) closes it. The catalogue of v 33-41 serves as a final audit, ensuring that every item commissioned in chs 25-31 has been executed and is ready for installation. The "behold, they had done it" of v 43 mirrors the "behold, it was very good" of Genesis 1:31, marking the completion of the tabernacle as the culmination of a new creative act — God creating, through his people's obedient labor, a dwelling for himself in the world.
32.theological_application: Matthew Henry's comparison of Moses's inspection to a "master-builder" surveying his work speaks to the accountability that governs all stewardship: what is built must correspond to what was commissioned. Paul extends this into the judgment seat of Christ: "each one's work will become manifest, for the Day will disclose it... each one will receive his wages according to his labor" (1 Cor 3:13-14). The blessing Moses pronounced over work faithfully done models the final blessing of the righteous: "Come, you who are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world" (Matt 25:34). Faithful execution of God's commission, according to God's pattern, ending in God's blessing — this is the tabernacle's narrative in miniature, and the shape of the Christian life in full.
32.christology: Moses's "behold, they had done it" over the completed tabernacle anticipates Jesus's "It is finished" (John 19:30) from the cross. The tabernacle's completion was followed immediately by the filling of God's glory (ch 40:34); the cross was followed by the resurrection, then Pentecost — the Spirit poured out as the new glory filling the new tabernacle of the church. Moses blessed the completed congregation; Christ, having ascended, sends the Spirit as the ongoing blessing on all who receive the completed work of redemption. The entire tabernacle project — from the first divine instructions in ch 25 to the final "so had they done it" in ch 39 — is the OT's most elaborate Christological type: the human work of faithful mediation preparing the dwelling-place for the divine glory to inhabit.
